name: Cycle Entry Point
description: Given a singly linked list of N nodes (2 <= N <= 1e5) that may have been tampered with to contain a cycle, implement `Address findCycleEntryPoint(List l)` declared in cycle.h. A cycle is where traversing NEXT repeatedly returns to an already-visited node. The function must return the address of the node where the cycle begins (its entry point), or NULL if the list is acyclic. The list is given via listlinier.h (FIRST, NEXT, INFO, Address, List).
rubric_structured:
1. Correctness (45%): Detects the cycle and returns its entry-point node; returns NULL when the list is acyclic.
   - 45/45 — Read as pseudocode on a small list whose tail links back to a known node (e.g. node 3), the returned address is exactly that entry node; on an acyclic list the traversal reaches the end and returns NULL.
   - 32/45 — Right idea with one cited bug — detects the cycle but returns the Floyd meeting point instead of resetting a pointer to the head to find the entry, or is off-by-one (returns the node just before/after the true entry), or forgets the acyclic -> NULL case.
   - 18/45 — Only partial — detects whether a cycle exists but never locates the entry, returns a node with no traceable reason, or works only when the entry happens to be the head.
   - 0/45 — Cannot be traced to a correct cycle-entry algorithm — there is no recognizable entry-finding strategy, the logic is broken or unrelated to the problem, or it returns uninitialised/garbage. (Compilation is NOT a factor: judge the algorithmic concept, not whether the code builds.)

2. Efficiency (25%): O(N) for N up to 1e5, without stack or memory blow-ups.
   - 25/25 — O(N) time and O(1) extra space — Floyd's two-pointer (slow/fast) for both detection and the entry-finding phase; no per-node inner scan, no large auxiliary buffer.
   - 18/25 — O(N) time but O(N) extra space — a visited set / marking pass (address array, or overwriting INFO with a sentinel). Correct and linear, just not the O(1)-space method.
   - 10/25 — Linear time but with a fixed stack buffer sized to N (e.g. `int arr[100005]` as a local) or O(N)-deep recursion that risks stack overflow at N=1e5.
   - 5/25 — O(N^2) — an inner loop that rescans visited nodes for every node, which would time out at N=1e5.

3. Cycle-detection approach & pointer handling (15%): A method that genuinely identifies the entry.
   - 15/15 — A sound entry-finding strategy end-to-end — Floyd detect-then-reset, or visited-marking that returns the first re-encountered node — with correct NEXT/pointer traversal.
   - 10/15 — Sound strategy but pointer handling is shaky in a cited spot — mixes `->next` with `NEXT()` inconsistently, or the second phase is fragile though it still lands on the entry for typical inputs.
   - 5/15 — Only half-formed — the detection and entry phases do not connect cleanly, or the marking/bookkeeping is visibly confused.
   - 0/15 — No coherent approach to locating the entry.

4. Code quality (15%): Readable C that respects the cycle.h contract.
   - 15/15 — Readable: the slow/fast (or visited/marker) variables have meaningful names, control flow is linear, and if the list is mutated (INFO overwritten as a marker) the original values are restored, honouring the I.S./F.S. contract.
   - 11/15 — One blemish you can cite — a leftover commented-out `main`, a destructive INFO overwrite that is never restored, or an unused variable/array.
   - 7/15 — Multiple style issues — single-letter names throughout, dead branches, or magic sentinels like `-123456789` with no explanation.
   - 3/15 — Hard to read end-to-end; you cannot quickly tell which pointer is which or how the entry is located.
rubric_unstructured: Score the submission on correctness (45%), efficiency (25%), cycle-detection approach and pointer handling (15%), and code quality (15%). Correctness is judged on the algorithm the code demonstrates: the function should return the entry-point node of the cycle and NULL when the list is acyclic. Returning the Floyd meeting point instead of resetting a pointer to the head to find the true entry is the most common partial-credit bug, and a lowercase `next()` where the header defines `NEXT` (won't compile) caps correctness. Efficiency rewards O(N) time; O(1) space via two pointers is ideal, an O(N)-space visited/marking pass is acceptable, while a fixed `arr[100005]` stack buffer or O(N) recursion that risks overflow at N=1e5, and any O(N^2) inner rescan, are progressively worse. Approach credit goes to a strategy that actually locates the entry with clean pointer traversal, not merely detecting that a cycle exists. Code quality covers readable names, linear control flow, respecting the cycle.h I.S./F.S. contract, and restoring the list if INFO is overwritten as a marker; a leftover commented-out `main`, unexplained magic sentinels, and destructive un-restored mutation are the typical deductions.
